title: The ERA Act, Open Science and the Freedom to Stay
content:
As the launch of the European Commission’s proposals for the European Research Area (ERA) Act get closer, there is growing interest in how it will look to deliver on its promise to make a reality of the “Fifth Freedom”: the free movement of knowledge.

This idea was most recently championed by former Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta in his report Much More than a Market. Letta identifies the movement of knowledge as a primary driver for EU competitiveness and hence one of the six key areas of action the EU should focus on.

Currently, discussions around this freedom focus heavily on researcher mobility, such as the recent proposals for model contracts designed to help

title: Copyright Maximalism Will Stifle a Research-Intensive Digital Economy
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 26 March 2026 on the Times Higher Education

On 18 March, after more than two years of deliberating what to do about artificial intelligence and copyright, the UK government has announced the need to “gather more evidence on how copyright laws are impacting the development and deployment of AI across the economy and the economic benefits of reform”.

Perhaps the reason for this delay has been minsters’ growing realisation that the issues concerning AI and copyright are far more complicated than the “Big Music v Big Tech” paradigm being presented in the media. As this

title: The Open Risk Facing UK Universities: A Lack of AI Sovereignty
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published in a shortened version on 11 March 2026 by Research Professional News

Universities make a uniquely important contribution to the UK’s research intensive economy, both through generating new ideas and insights through basic research, and then through their application, for example in the form of research–industry collaborations, spinouts and more.

In order to maximise productivity, like almost every other sector, the research sector is looking at how to make the most of AI technologies. Closely connected to this, UK researchers are also working to develop new models and applications of AI that can support numerous sectors of the economy including the NHS, the environment, agriculture etc.

In this context, as we see with the ongoing furore around Palantir, it is a cause for concern that we are so dependent on US technology

title: Cyprus Workshop Report: The Secondary Publication Right
content:
Blog post by Giorgos Glossiotis

On Monday, 16 March 2026, KR21 organised a workshop, with the support of the Cyprus Association of Librarians – Information Scientists (CALIS, ΚΕΒΕΠ), titled “The Secondary Publication Right: Concept, Legal Framework, and the Bulgarian Experience”, which was held in Nicosia, at the University of Cyprus Library. The event was led by Ana Lazarova, KR21 national coordinator for Bulgaria.

Following a warm welcome from CALIS President Theodora Petrou, participants were introduced to KR21’s new campaign, “Let Libraries be Libraries,” by Giorgos Glossiotis, KR21 national coordinator for Greece and Cyprus.

Deep Dive: Secondary Publishing Rights & the Bulgarian Experience

title: 14 April Webinar | Closed Licences, Closed Doors: Open Licensing, AI, and the Future of Research Impact
content:
When?

  • Date: 14 April 2026
  • Time: 11:00 – 12:30 CEST / 10:00 – 11:30 BST

What?

Open licensing and copyright are important aspects of publishing research, but what do they mean in practice? Creative Commons (CC) licences offer a standardised framework for sharing research outputs, with open access being increasingly mandated by international funders. However, growing concern about research outputs being used to train generative AI tools has led some authors to seek more restrictive licences. Yet the question remains: Does a more restrictive licence stop AI from using your work, or does it simply limit the collaborations driving innovation?

Europe PMC, partners of EMBL-EBI, and KR21 invite you to a joint webinar tackling this pressing question.

Save Your Spot!

title: Portuguese Civil Society Proposes Secondary Publication Rights and Obligation
content:
Blog post by Eduardo Leonardo dos Santos

Portuguese translation available here

On 5 February, the magnificent Great Hall of the prestigious Academy of Sciences of Lisbon hosted Portugal’s first conference in Portugal on Secondary Publication Rights (SPR). Not even an extraordinary storm that struck Lisbon for several days could prevent an afternoon of lively discussion on the role legislation should play in advancing Open Access to publicly funded scientific outputs.

The event was organised by civil society organisations Knowledge Rights 21, COMMUNIA, and the Portuguese Association of Librarians, Archivists and Information and Documentation Professionals, alongside three Universities: NOVA University Lisbon, Coimbra University, and Minho University, with support from OPERAS and the Academy of Sciences of Lisbo

title: Two Europes in Five Minutes
content:
Within a few minutes of each other yesterday in Strasbourg, Members of the European Parliament voted through two motions relevant to how we regulate knowledge creation, sharing and application to support research and innovation. Neither has any immediate legal impact, but still set a direction that may well shape future, legislative, measures, for better or for worse. 

And that direction is… confused. 

The first to be voted on was a report on Generative AI and Copyright, prepared on the initiative of the Parliament’s Legal Affairs Committee, and led by Axel Voss MEP. The second was a motion for resolution on the upcoming European Research Area Act, led by Eszter Lakos MEP.  

This blog explores what each says about how the EU should regulate knowledge, and where the risk of confusion arises. 

The Texts

Axel

title: The UK’s Copyright Laws Will Hobble Its AI Ambitions
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 11 December 2025 on the Times Higher Education

Artificial intelligence played a crucial role in enabling the safe and rapid development and roll-out of the Covid vaccine. Using scientific journal articles, published clinical trials, databases and other resources, scientists were able to use machine learning models to quickly prioritise fruitful research pathways, allowing them to bypass traditional bottlenecks associated with vaccine development and enhance the precision of antigen selection.

In the US, where Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna developed the mRNA vaccine, the relevant data laws support such use of information by commercial entities and universities alike. But under UK copyright law, AstraZeneca scientists – who co-developed the Oxford-AstraZeneca viral vector vaccine – would only have been allowed to “mine” the tens if not hundreds of thousands of potentially r
